The change modifies how the nextLocal variable is calculated by changing instantLocal - offsetLocal to instantLocal + offsetLocal.

The output format requires me to return a JSON object with "mutant_id" and "is_fault_adjacent". I remember that fault adjacent mutants are those where a single line of code has two mutations, potentially causing issues because they might interfere with each other. Looking at the mutant code changes, it seems only one line was altered: line 340. So, there's only one mutation here.

Next, I check the failing tests information. One test (TestMonthDay_Properties) went from failing to passing, which is good. However, several other tests either remained failing or started failing after the mutation. This indicates that changing line 340 might have introduced new bugs because it's a critical part of handling time zone transitions.

Since only one mutant was applied and no adjacent mutations are present, the "is_fault_adjacent" should be false. Fault adjacency would require two consecutive mutations on the same line or nearby lines, which isn't the case here.

Putting it all together, the mutant is not fault-adjacent because there's only a single mutation, so the JSON response will reflect that.
